addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

## claude.ai Dropbox
Use Dropbox MCP server.

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Use this server whenever the user wants to create, generate, edit, implement, or sync any design, UI, screen, component, mockup, or visual — in Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or Figma Slides — and whenever the user mentions Figma or provides a figma.com URL.

This server bridges code and design in both directions, and supports designing from scratch using existing design systems and codebases.

CAPABILITIES:
- Read designs FROM Figma into code (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Write designs INTO Figma from code, intent, or existing components (use_figma, generate_figma_design, create_new_file, upload_assets)
- Bridge code and design via Code Connect (get_code_connect_map, add_code_connect_map)
- Create diagrams and FigJam content (generate_diagram, get_figjam)

WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user wants to create, mock up, or generate any UI, screen, component, or design — even if Figma isn't named
- The user wants to implement a design as code (design-to-code)
- The user wants to push a page, view, or component into Figma (code-to-design)
- The user wants to update, sync, or edit an existing Figma file
- The user shares a figma.com URL
- The user wants to build or extend a design system, design tokens, or component library
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

SKILLS (Prefer the skills shipped with the Figma plugin on the agent being used. If no Figma plugin is installed, use the skills served by the Figma MCP resource):
- /figma-use — MANDATORY before calling use_figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-use/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-design — for translating an app page or layout into Figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-design/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-library — for building a design system in Figma from code; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-library/SKILL.md
- /figma-code-connect — for mapping Figma components to codebase components; fallback: skill://figma/figma-code-connect/SKILL.md
- /figma-use-figjam — for FigJam-specific use_f… [truncated]

## claude.ai Uber
MCP server for rides-3p-demand-mcp service

## claude.ai Zapier
This server connects you to 9,000+ apps via Zapier.

IMPORTANT: Before calling any tool, ensure you have loaded the available tools from this server (tools/list).

If the user seems new or has no tools enabled, retrieve the "zapier:onboarding" skill using get_zapier_skill and follow its instructions.
Use list_zapier_skills to discover other saved workflows.

Before executing any action, always call list_enabled_zapier_actions first.
If an app isn't enabled, use discover_zapier_actions then enable_zapier_action to add it.

attachment.content:
- supacode-cli: Control Supacode from the terminal. Use when running Supacode CLI commands, managing worktrees, tabs, and surfaces programmatically, or when inside a Supacode terminal session.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

-`192.168.7.153:11434` so LAN clients (Claude Code via ollama-code-mcp, the
+Key choices: Tesla V100 (32 GB VRAM) pinned by GPU UUID; flash attention +
+q8_0 KV cache to fit larger contexts; `OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=65536`;
+`OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=2` (2 concurrent requests per loaded model); LoadBalancer
+at `192.168.7.153:11434` so LAN clients (Claude Code via ollama-code-mcp, the
 Xcode instance) reach it directly. 100Gi Longhorn PV for models.
+
+## Context length
+
+`OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=65536` was set on 2026-07-18 because the hermes-agent
+(`10-hermes`) needs at least 64k. Without it Ollama auto-sized the window to
+32768, while hermes auto-detected 262144 from the model metadata and packed
+prompts to fit — every response came back with
+`finish_reason='length'` (`⚠️ Response truncated`).
+
+VRAM budget on `qwen3.6:35b` (36B MoE, Q4_K_M): weights occupy 21692 MiB of
+the 32768 MiB card with all 42 layers on GPU, leaving ~11 GB for KV cache and
+compute buffers. Raising context further, or loading a denser model alongside,
+risks CPU offload — check `ollama ps` (the `CONTEXT` column) and `nvidia-smi`
+after any change.
+
+Unresolved: whether `OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=2` allocates 65536 per slot or splits
+it across both. If it splits, clients see 32k and the truncation returns.

content.content:
1	# Fleet GitOps Handoff — Service Configs for k3s Cluster
2	
3	**Purpose:** This document gives you (the local LLM) the information needed to help
4	write Fleet-ready GitOps configs for the listed services and commit them to the
5	`homelab-fleet` GitHub repo via the GitHub MCP tools.
6	
7	**Cluster basics:**
8	- Single-node k3s on openSUSE Leap 16 host (Podman, not Docker).
9	- Host LAN IP: `192.168.7.149`. MetalLB pool on the `192.168.7.x` range.
10	- Storage class: `longhorn`. Most app namespace: `ai`.
11	- GPUs: Tesla V100 (`GPU-a69c6398-0353-3234-9f35-af0e02865f33`),
12	  GTX 1070 (`GPU-5641c03a-6749-62ab-cd11-fd3cd34d29f4`).
13	
14	---
15	
16	## ⚠️ CRITICAL — Two categories of service
17	
18	The services below fall into two groups. **Treat them differently.**
19	
20	### Group A — configs are KNOWN (provided in this doc)
21	These were built recently and their manifests are documented here accurately:
22	`node-red`, `resilio`, `emby`, `k8s-mcp-server`.
23	
24	For these: the config details in this doc are reliable. You can use them to write
25	Fleet manifests. Still verify against the live cluster with the k8s MCP tools
26	before committing (image tags or tweaks may have changed).
27	
28	### Group B — configs are NOT known (you MUST read them from the cluster)
29	These were deployed BEFORE this info was captured. **The details are NOT provided
30	because they are not known.** Do NOT invent or guess values for these:
31	`ollama`, `milvus`, `attu`, `open-webui`.
32	
33	For these you MUST pull the real running config from the cluster using the
34	Kubernetes MCP tools (read-only). Steps:
35	1. Use the k8s MCP tools to `listResources` / `getResource` for Deployments,
36	   StatefulSets, Services, PVCs, ConfigMaps in the `ai` namespace matching each app.
37	2. For Helm-deployed services (likely `ollama` and `milvus`), the rendered
38	   manifests are what the cluster shows — but the GitOps source-of-truth is the
39	   Helm values. Flag to the user that Helm values must be captured with
40	   `helm -n ai get values <release>` (you may not be able to run helm via the
41	   read-only k8s MCP — tell the user to run it and paste the output).
42	3. STRIP runtime fields before writing to Git: `status`, `metadata.uid`,
43	   `metadata.resourceVersion`, `metadata.creationTimestamp`,
44	   `metadata.managedFields`, `spec.clusterIP` (let it re-allocate), and any
45	   `ownerReferences`. These do not belong in GitOps source.
46	
47	**Do not fabricate Group B configs. If you cannot read them, say so and ask the
48	user to provide them. A wrong GitOps config will be applied to the cluster by
49	Fleet.**
50	
51	---
52	
53	## Group A — Known configs
54	
55	### node-red
56	- Namespace: `node-red` (dedicated). Image: `nodered/node-red:latest`.
57	- Storage: Longhorn PVC `node-red-data` (RWO, 5Gi) mounted at `/data`.
58	- Runs as uid/gid 1000; pod securityContext `runAsUser/Group/fsGroup: 1000`
59	  (the official image takes runAsUser cleanly).
60	- Env: `TZ=America/Chicago`.
61	- Port: 1880. Service: LoadBalancer (MetalLB).
62	- Strategy: Recreate (single RWO writer).
63	- Note: editor is unauthenticated by default — user should set adminAuth in
64	  /data/settings.js.
65	
66	### resilio (Resilio Sync)
67	- Namespace: `resilio`. Image: `resilio/sync:latest`.
68	- Storage: hostPath `/data/resilio` (restored prior deployment — config + identity).
69	- Key in-container paths (from the restored sync.conf):
70	  - storage_path = `/mnt/sync/config`  (host `/data/resilio/config`)
71	  - folders whitelist = `/mnt/sync/folders` (host `/data/resilio/folders`)
72	  - config file = `/mnt/sync/sync.conf` (host `/data/resilio/sync.conf`)
73	  - webui listen = `0.0.0.0:8888`; listening_port = 55555
74	- On-disk ownership uid 471 / gid 100 -> PUID/PGID and runAsUser must match.
75	- Command: `rslsync --nodaemon --config /mnt/sync/sync.conf`.
76	- Ports: 8888 (webui), 55555 (sync). Service: LoadBalancer (MetalLB).
77	- Strategy: Recreate (single hostPath writer).
78	- Mount the whole tree subdirs to match sync.conf's expected in-container paths.
79	
80	### emby (Emby Media Server)
81	- Namespace: `emby`. Image: `emby/embyserver:latest`.
82	- This is an s6-overlay image: it MUST start as root and drop to UID/GID itself.
83	  DO NOT set a pod-level runAsUser/securityContext — it breaks s6 setup. Use env
84	  `UID`/`GID` (and `PUID`/`PGID`) = 472 / 473 instead.
85	- Storage (hostPath, restored prior deployment):
86	  - host `/data/emby`   -> container `/config`  (config/db, owner 472:473)
87	  - host `/data/stream` -> container `/data/stream` (media; DB expects this exact
88	    path — 1:1 mount keeps libraries intact)
89	- GPU transcode: GTX 1070 pinned via `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=GPU-5641c03a-...`
90	  with `NVIDIA_DRIVER_CAPABILITIES=compute,utility,video` (the `video` cap is
91	  required for NVENC/NVDEC). `runtimeClassName: nvidia`, `nvidia.com/gpu: 1`.
92	- Ports: 8096 (http), 8920 (https). Service: LoadBalancer (MetalLB).

# Fleet GitOps Handoff — Service Configs for k3s Cluster

**Purpose:** This document gives you (the local LLM) the information needed to help
write Fleet-ready GitOps configs for the listed services and commit them to the
`homelab-fleet` GitHub repo via the GitHub MCP tools.

**Cluster basics:**
- Single-node k3s on openSUSE Leap 16 host (Podman, not Docker).
- Host LAN IP: `192.168.7.149`. MetalLB pool on the `192.168.7.x` range.
- Storage class: `longhorn`. Most app namespace: `ai`.
- GPUs: Tesla V100 (`GPU-a69c6398-0353-3234-9f35-af0e02865f33`),
  GTX 1070 (`GPU-5641c03a-6749-62ab-cd11-fd3cd34d29f4`).

---

## ⚠️ CRITICAL — Two categories of service

The services below fall into two groups. **Treat them differently.**

### Group A — configs are KNOWN (provided in this doc)
These were built recently and their manifests are documented here accurately:
`node-red`, `resilio`, `emby`, `k8s-mcp-server`.

For these: the config details in this doc are reliable. You can use them to write
Fleet manifests. Still verify against the live cluster with the k8s MCP tools
before committing (image tags or tweaks may have changed).

### Group B — configs are NOT known (you MUST read them from the cluster)
These were deployed BEFORE this info was captured. **The details are NOT provided
because they are not known.** Do NOT invent or guess values for these:
`ollama`, `milvus`, `attu`, `open-webui`.

For these you MUST pull the real running config from the cluster using the
Kubernetes MCP tools (read-only). Steps:
1. Use the k8s MCP tools to `listResources` / `getResource` for Deployments,
   StatefulSets, Services, PVCs, ConfigMaps in the `ai` namespace matching each app.
2. For Helm-deployed services (likely `ollama` and `milvus`), the rendered
   manifests are what the cluster shows — but the GitOps source-of-truth is the
   Helm values. Flag to the user that Helm values must be captured with
   `helm -n ai get values <release>` (you may not be able to run helm via the
   read-only k8s MCP — tell the user to run it and paste the output).
3. STRIP runtime fields before writing to Git: `status`, `metadata.uid`,
   `metadata.resourceVersion`, `metadata.creationTimestamp`,
   `metadata.managedFields`, `spec.clusterIP` (let it re-allocate), and any
   `ownerReferences`. These do not belong in GitOps source.

**Do not fabricate Group B configs. If you cannot read them, say so and ask the
user to provide them. A wrong GitOps config will be applied to the cluster by
Fleet.**

---

## Group A — Known configs

### node-red
- Namespace: `node-red` (dedicated). Image: `nodered/node-red:latest`.
- Storage: Longhorn PVC `node-red-data` (RWO, 5Gi) mounted at `/data`.
- Runs as uid/gid 1000; pod securityContext `runAsUser/Group/fsGroup: 1000`
  (the official image takes runAsUser cleanly).
- Env: `TZ=America/Chicago`.
- Port: 1880. Service: LoadBalancer (MetalLB).
- Strategy: Recreate (single RWO writer).
- Note: editor is unauthenticated by default — user should set adminAuth in
  /data/settings.js.

### resilio (Resilio Sync)
- Namespace: `resilio`. Image: `resilio/sync:latest`.
- Storage: hostPath `/data/resilio` (restored prior deployment — config + identity).
- Key in-container paths (from the restored sync.conf):
  - storage_path = `/mnt/sync/config`  (host `/data/resilio/config`)
  - folders whitelist = `/mnt/sync/folders` (host `/data/resilio/folders`)
  - config file = `/mnt/sync/sync.conf` (host `/data/resilio/sync.conf`)
  - webui listen = `0.0.0.0:8888`; listening_port = 55555
- On-disk ownership uid 471 / gid 100 -> PUID/PGID and runAsUser must match.
- Command: `rslsync --nodaemon --config /mnt/sync/sync.conf`.
- Ports: 8888 (webui), 55555 (sync). Service: LoadBalancer (MetalLB).
- Strategy: Recreate (single hostPath writer).
- Mount the whole tree subdirs to match sync.conf's expected in-container paths.

### emby (Emby Media Server)
- Namespace: `emby`. Image: `emby/embyserver:latest`.
- This is an s6-overlay image: it MUST start as root and drop to UID/GID itself.
  DO NOT set a pod-level runAsUser/securityContext — it breaks s6 setup. Use env
  `UID`/`GID` (and `PUID`/`PGID`) = 472 / 473 instead.
- Storage (hostPath, restored prior deployment):
  - host `/data/emby`   -> container `/config`  (config/db, owner 472:473)
  - host `/data/stream` -> container `/data/stream` (media; DB expects this exact
    path — 1:1 mount keeps libraries intact)
- GPU transcode: GTX 1070 pinned via `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=GPU-5641c03a-...`
  with `NVIDIA_DRIVER_CAPABILITIES=compute,utility,video` (the `video` cap is
  required for NVENC/NVDEC). `runtimeClassName: nvidia`, `nvidia.com/gpu: 1`.
- Ports: 8096 (http), 8920 (https). Service: LoadBalancer (MetalLB).
- Strategy: Recreate.

### k8s-mcp-server
- Namespace: `ai`. Image: `ginnux/k8s-mcp-server:latest`.
- Deployed READ-ONLY: `--mode streamable-http --port 8080 --read-only`.
- ServiceAccount `k8s-mcp-server-sa` + a read-only ClusterRole (get/list/watch
  only; no create/update/patch/delete) + ClusterRoleBinding.
- Service: ClusterIP on 8080 (in-cluster; reached by mcpo).
- Bridged to Open-WebUI via mcpo (OpenAPI), not connected directly.

---

## Group B — Read these from the cluster (NOT provided; do not guess)

For each, use the k8s MCP tools to read the live objects in namespace `ai`, then
strip runtime fields (listed above) before writing Fleet manifests.

### ollama
- Deployed via Helm (chart `ollama-helm/ollama`). Pinned to the V100 by UUID.
- KNOWN hints (verify against cluster, do not treat as complete):
  - Engine pinned to V100 via `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=GPU-a69c6398-...`
  - Service type LoadBalancer; PVC ~100Gi Longhorn; serves :11434.
  - Models are stored on the PVC, not in config (don't need to encode them).
- ACTION: get Helm values (`helm -n ai get values ollama`) for the GitOps source,
  AND read the rendered Deployment/Service/PVC for reference. Prefer a Fleet
  HelmRelease (chart + values) over raw manifests for this one.

### milvus
- Deployed via Helm (standalone, v2.6.x). Backed by Longhorn PVCs (data, etcd,
  minio). Service on :19530.
- ACTION: get Helm values (`helm -n ai get values milvus`) and read the rendered
  resources. Prefer a Fleet HelmRelease. Do NOT hand-write Milvus manifests —
  it's a multi-component chart.

### attu
- Milvus admin UI. Image `zilliz/attu` (version is independent of Milvus version).
- ACTION: read the live Deployment + Service from the cluster. Likely a simple
  Deployment + Service; capture exact image tag and the MILVUS_URL env it uses.

### open-webui
- Deployed as a StatefulSet (pod `open-webui-0`), NOT a Deployment. Has a Longhorn
  PVC. Serves the chat UI. Has `WEBUI_SECRET_KEY` set (required so stored tool
  credentials survive restarts).
- ACTION: read the live StatefulSet + Service + PVC. Capture the exact image tag,
  env (especially WEBUI_SECRET_KEY handling — keep the secret in a k8s Secret,
  NOT inline in Git), and the PVC name. Flag any secrets to the user to store
  via a Secret, not committed plaintext.

---

## Fleet repo structure (suggested)

Target repo: `homelab-fleet`. A common Fleet layout:

```
homelab-fleet/
  fleet.yaml                 # top-level bundle config (optional defaults)
  node-red/
    fleet.yaml
    node-red.yaml
  resilio/
    fleet.yaml
    resilio-sync.yaml
  emby/
    fleet.yaml
    emby-server.yaml
  k8s-mcp-server/
    fleet.yaml
    k8s-mcp-server.yaml
  ollama/
    fleet.yaml               # HelmRelease-style: chart ref + values
  milvus/
    fleet.yaml               # HelmRelease-style
  attu/
    fleet.yaml
    attu.yaml
  open-webui/
    fleet.yaml
    open-webui.yaml
```

Each app dir gets a `fleet.yaml` telling Fleet how to deploy that path. For Helm
apps (ollama, milvus), the `fleet.yaml` references the chart + values rather than
raw manifests.

---

## Hard rules for writing these configs

1. **Never invent Group B values.** Read them from the cluster. If you can't, ask.
2. **Strip runtime fields** (status, uid, resourceVersion, creationTimestamp,
   managedFields, clusterIP, ownerReferences) from anything read via kubectl/MCP.
3. **Secrets do NOT go in Git as plaintext.** Any token/password/secret-key
   (e.g. open-webui's WEBUI_SECRET_KEY, the github-mcp-token) must be a k8s Secret
   referenced by name — flag these to the user to create/seal separately
   (e.g. SealedSecrets or sops) rather than committing values.
4. **Helm services use HelmRelease/values**, not rendered manifests, as GitOps
   source.
5. **Spot-check before commit.** Fleet reconciles Git -> cluster, so a wrong file
   gets applied. Summarize what each file will do before writing it.
6. Use `create_or_update_file` or `push_files` to write to `homelab-fleet`. You do
   NOT have repo-create, fork, or file-delete tools (deliberately excluded).

Every file was curated from **live cluster state** — helm values pulled from running releases, manifests reconstructed from applied objects, and application sources recovered from container image layers after their Dockerfiles were lost. 4 5 This repo is mounted as the [`lab/` submodule of ash4d.com](https://github.com/darthzen/ash4d.com), which carries the full architecture documentation. This repo carries the configs. 6 7 ## Installation Order 8 9 ```mermaid 10 flowchart TD 11 subgraph host["00 — Host (openSUSE Leap 16.0)"] 12 DRIVER[NVIDIA G06 driver + CDI] 13 K3S[k3s v1.35] 14 end 15 16 subgraph platform["01-03 — Platform"] 17 METALLB[01 MetalLB

+ Device Plugin + DCGM] 20 end 21 22 subgraph ai["04-09 — AI Stack (namespace: ai)"] 23 OLLAMA[04 Ollama
Qwen3 / V100] 24 OWUI[05 Open WebUI] 25 MILVUS[06 Milvus + Attu] 26 COMFY[07 ComfyUI] 27 INDEXER[08 k8s-docs-indexer] 28 MCP[09 MCP layer
mcpo · k8s · github · retrieval] 29 end 30 31 subgraph apps["10-13 — Applications"] 32 HERMES[10 Hermes Agent] 33 EMBY[11 Emby] 34 NODERED[12 Node-RED] 35 RESILIO[13 Resilio Sync] 36 end 37 38 subgraph mgmt["14 — Management Plane"] 39 RANCHER[Rancher downstream
Fleet · Monitoring] 40 end 41 42 host --> platform --> ai --> apps 43 platform --> mgmt 44 45 classDef ai_c fill:#059669,stroke:#047857,color:#fff 46 class OLLAMA,OWUI,MILVUS,COMFY,INDEXER,MCP,HERMES ai_c 47 ``` 48 49 ## Node Specifications 50 51 | Resource | Details | 52 |---|---| 53 | **Node** | Single-node k3s (`sdf1`) on openSUSE Leap 16.0 | 54 | **k3s Version** | v1.35.5+k3s1 (containerd 2.2.3-k3s1) | 55 | **GPU 0** | NVIDIA Tesla V100 32GB — LLM inference, pinned by UUID to Ollama | 56 | **GPU 1** | NVIDIA GTX 1070 8GB — ComfyUI (device plugin) + Emby transcode (UUID pin) | 57 | **Load Balancer Pool** | MetalLB L2, `192.168.7.150-169` | 58 | **Storage** | Longhorn CSI across all stateful workloads | 59 60 ## Components 61 62 | Step | Component | Chart / Source | Version | 63 |---|---|---|---| 64 | 00 | Host prep (NVIDIA G06, CDI, k3s) | zypper / get.k3s.io | k3s v1.35.5+k3s1 | 65 | 01 | MetalLB + Traefik | metallb/metallb; k3s-bundled traefik | 0.16.1 / 39.0.7 (v3.6.12) | 66 | 02 | Longhorn | longhorn/longhorn | 1.12.0 | 67 | 03 | GPU (RuntimeClass, device plugin, DCGM) | nvdp/nvidia-device-plugin | 0.19.2 / dcgm 4.8.2 | 68 | 04 | Ollama | otwld ollama-helm | chart 1.60.0 (app 0.30.6) | 69 | 05 | Open WebUI | open-webui/open-webui | chart 14.8.0 (app 0.9.6) | 70 | 06 | Milvus (+ Attu UI) | zilliztech/milvus | chart 5.0.22 (app 2.6.18) | 71 | 07 | ComfyUI (+ filebrowser) | mmartial/comfyui-nvidia-docker | latest | 72 | 08 | k8s-docs-indexer | local image — source in-repo | v2 | 73 | 09 | MCP layer (mcpo, k8s/github MCP, retrieval-tool) | manifests + in-repo source | — | 74 | 10 | Hermes agent (Slack AI agent) | nousresearch/hermes-agent | latest | 75 | 11 | Emby media server (GPU transcode) | emby/embyserver | latest | 76 | 12 | Node-RED home automation | nodered/node-red | latest | 77 | 13 | Resilio Sync (P2P file sync) | resilio/sync | latest | 78 | 14 | Cluster mgmt plane (Rancher downstream) | README only | v2.14.2 | 79 80 Each directory contains a README with the exact install commands and the reasoning behind non-default values. Helm components follow one pattern: 81 82 ```bash 83 helm upgrade --install -n --create-namespace -f /values.yaml 84 ``` 85 86 ## How This Repo Was Built 87 88 ```mermaid 89 flowchart LR 90 CLUSTER[("Live k3s cluster
(source of truth)")] 91 DUMP[dump-lab-state.sh
helm values + manifests] 92 FORENSICS[recover-image-sources.sh
OCI layer forensics] 93 CURATE[Curation
clean · sanitize · document] 94 REPO[("lab-fleet")] 95 96 CLUSTER --> DUMP --> CURATE 97 CLUSTER --> FORENSICS --> CURATE 98 CURATE --> REPO 99 100 classDef tool fill:#059669,stroke:#047857,color:#fff 101 class DUMP,FORENSICS tool 102 ``` 103 104 Configuration was captured from the running cluster rather than from build-time notes, so it reflects what actually works — including fixes discovered in production (see the emptyDir EBUSY note in `08-indexer/`). The two locally-built images had lost their Dockerfiles entirely; their sources and build recipes were recovered by mounting the image snapshots and reconstructing build history from OCI layer metadata. 105 106 **Sanitization:** no kubeconfigs, tokens, or secret values live in this repo. Secrets are referenced via `secretKeyRef` with documented `kubectl create secret` commands and `.example` templates. 107 108 ## Related Repositories 109 110 - [`ash4d.com`](https://github.com/darthzen/ash4d.com) — architecture documentation, deployment plan, and public site; this repo is its `lab/` submodule 111 - [`ollama-code-mcp`](https://github.com/darthzen/ollama-code-mcp) — MCP server delegating Claude Code tasks to the Ollama instance defined here 112 113 ## About 114 115 Built by [Rick Ashford](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rickashford/) — Sales Engineering leader with 17 years at SUSE, specializing in Kubernetes, Linux, AI/LLM platforms, and open-source ecosystem strategy. 116

I read the handoff (`~/Downloads/fleet-handoff.md`), and before executing anything I need to flag something important: **the handoff's work already appears to be done — and superseded by the repo you're sitting in.**

## What the handoff asks for

It's a doc written for a *local LLM* to author "Fleet-ready GitOps configs" for 8 services — `node-red`, `resilio`, `emby`, `k8s-mcp-server`, `ollama`, `milvus`, `attu`, `open-webui` — and **push them to a separate `homelab-fleet` GitHub repo via GitHub MCP tools**.

## What actually exists

Every one of those 8 services already has config-as-code in **this** `lab-fleet` repo:

| Handoff target | Already in repo |
|---|---|
| node-red | `12-node-red/node-red.yaml` |
| resilio | `13-resilio/resilio.yaml` |
| emby | `11-emby/emby.yaml` |
| k8s-mcp-server | `09-mcp/k8s-mcp-server.yaml` |
| ollama (Helm) | `04-ollama/values.yaml` |
| milvus (Helm) | `06-milvus/values.yaml` |
| attu | `06-milvus/attu.yaml` |
| open-webui | `05-open-webui/values.yaml` |

The initial commit — *"install-ordered lab config-as-code (00-14), curated from live cluster state"* — is essentially the executed (and improved) result of this handoff.

## Why I stopped instead of executing

Taking the handoff literally would mean creating a **redundant, separate `homelab-fleet` repo** and re-deriving configs that already exist here (more current — e.g. the V100 is now documented as 32 GB, ollama values were bumped today). It also can't be done as written: the git remote here is `.../gh/darthzen/lab-fleet` (not `homelab-fleet`), no GitHub MCP tools are loaded, and pushing a new public repo is an outward-facing action I shouldn't take on a stale doc without checking.

**One arguable gap:** the handoff wanted true Fleet *bundle* structure (a `fleet.yaml` per app). This repo ships plain manifests + Helm `values.yaml` (the `helm upgrade --install` pattern), not Fleet `fleet.yaml` wrappers — even though step 14 references a Rancher/Fleet mgmt plane.

How do you want to proceed?

content.content:
1 # Fleet Wiring Plan — lab-fleet → Rancher Continuous Delivery 2 3 **Date:** 2026-07-06 4 **Status:** Plan — grounded in live cluster discovery, nothing applied 5 **Goal:** turn `lab-fleet` from published reference config into **functional GitOps** — Rancher Fleet actually deploys it to the ash4d cluster. This is the still-open half of the scope doc's A/B question and the stronger portfolio line ("and Fleet deploys from this repo"). 6 7 --- 8 9 ## Discovered state (live, 2026-07-06) 10 11 Pulled read-only from the downstream API (`192.168.7.149:6443`, `system:admin` cert) and the cluster-agent env. 12 13 | Fact | Value | Source | 14 |---|---|---| 15 | Rancher server | `v2.14.2` at `https://rancher.ash4d.com` (**192.168.7.148** — separate host) | cattle-cluster-agent env `CATTLE_SERVER` / `CATTLE_SERVER_VERSION` | 16 | ash4d lab node | `sdf1`, k3s `v1.35.5+k3s1`, single node, `node-role…control-plane=true` | `/version`, `/api/v1/nodes` | 17 | Cluster role | **Downstream** of Rancher (cluster id `c-nnzn9`), cattle-cluster-agent + fleet-agent connected | helm release `fleet-agent-c-nnzn9`, cluster-agent env | 18 | fleet-agent | `rancher/fleet-agent:v0.15.2`, Deployment **Ready 1/1** | `cattle-fleet-system` deploys | 19 | **Active GitOps here** | **None** — no `fleet.cattle.io` CRDs on the downstream, no `cluster-fleet-*` namespaces, no BundleDeployments | `/apis/fleet.cattle.io/v1alpha1` → 404; CRD + namespace lists | 20 | Feature hint | cluster-agent env shows `CATTLE_FEATURES=…fleet=false…` | cattle-cluster-agent env | 21 22 **Read:** the cluster is registered to Rancher and the fleet-agent is healthy, but Continuous Delivery has never been wired — no repo deploys here. GitRepo/Bundle objects live on the **controller** (the Rancher local cluster, `.148`), which is why none appear on the downstream. 23 24 > ⚠ **Assumption flagged:** `fleet=false` in the *agent* env is a strong hint that Continuous Delivery is disabled globally, but the authoritative flag lives on the controller (`.148`), which I can't reach without a Rancher token. Phase 0 verifies it before anything else. 25 26 --- 27 28 ## Topology 29 30 ```mermaid 31 flowchart LR 32 subgraph mgmt["Rancher server — 192.168.7.148 (rancher.ash4d.com)"] 33 RANCHER["Rancher v2.14.2
local cluster"] 34 FC["Fleet controller
+ gitjob"] 35 GR["GitRepo CR
(fleet-default)"] 36 RANCHER --- FC --- GR 37 end 38 subgraph down["ash4d lab — 192.168.7.149 (sdf1, downstream c-nnzn9)"] 39 AGENT["fleet-agent v0.15.2
(Ready, idle today)"] 40 BD["BundleDeployments
(none yet)"] 41 WL["cluster workloads
ai / longhorn / etc."] 42 AGENT --> BD --> WL 43 end 44 GH[("GitHub
darthzen/lab-fleet")] 45 GR -- "clones + renders" --> GH 46 FC == "pushes Bundles over the
registered agent tunnel" ==> AGENT 47 ``` 48 49 --- 50 51 ## Key design decisions (made, with rationale) 52 53 1. **Point the GitRepo at `lab-fleet` directly — not `ash4d.com/lab`.** 54 `ash4d.com` mounts `lab-fleet` as a git *submodule*; Fleet's git clone does not initialise submodules reliably, so a GitRepo on `ash4d.com/lab` would clone an empty gitlink. Targeting `lab-fleet` directly sidesteps this. `ash4d.com` stays the documentation showcase; `lab-fleet` is the deploy source. (This resolves the scope doc's A/B toward "split, deploy from lab-fleet.") 55 56 2. **Public repo → no clone secret.** `lab-fleet` is public, so the GitRepo needs no SSH/HTTP credential. One less secret on the controller. 57 58 3. **Stage by risk; adopt hand-installed Helm releases last.** Everything in `lab-fleet` was hand-installed. Fleet taking over a live Helm release is the only genuinely risky step (ownership handoff, possible pod churn). Raw-manifest components go first; storage/GPU/critical infra goes last or stays monitor-only. 59 60 4. **`lab-fleet` needs `fleet.yaml` files to be deployable.** Today it holds helm *values* and raw manifests, not Fleet bundles. Each component dir needs a `fleet.yaml` (helm chart+version+valuesFiles, or a raw-manifest bundle). This is the bulk of the repo work and is agent-executable under the existing ticket workflow. 61 62 --- 63 64 ## Phase 0 — Controller access + enable CD (owner: Rick; ~15 min) 65 66 Needs the Rancher control plane (`.148`), which the sandbox can't authenticate to. 67 68 1. **Mint a Rancher API token** — UI: avatar → *Account & API Keys* → *Create API Key* (no scope = all clusters), or: 69 ```bash 70 # login → bearer token 71 curl -sk https://rancher.ash4d.com/v3-public/localProviders/local?action=login \ 72 -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \ 73 -d '{"username":"admin","password":""}' | jq -r .token # -> token-xxxxx:secret 74 export RTOKEN='token-xxxxx:secret' 75 ``` 76 2. **Verify the fleet feature is on** (the agent env hinted `fleet=false`): 77 ```bash 78 curl -sk https://rancher.ash4d.com/v3/features/fleet -H "Authorization: Bearer $RTOKEN" | jq '{name:.id,value:.value,default:.default}' 79 # if disabled, enable: 80 curl -sk -X PUT https://rancher.ash4d.com/v3/features/fleet \ 81 -H "Authorization: Bearer $RTOKEN" -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \ 82 -d '{"value":true}' 83 ``` 84 Enabling installs the Fleet controller/CRDs and the CD area in the UI. Give it a minute to reconcile. 85 3. **Confirm the cluster's Fleet identity + labels** (for targeting): 86 ```bash 87 curl -sk https://rancher.ash4d.com/v1/fleet.cattle.io.clusters \ 88 -H "Authorization: Bearer $RTOKEN" | jq '.data[]|{ns:.metadata.namespace,name:.metadata.name,labels:.metadata.labels,cluster:.status.agentDeployedComponents}' 89 # note the workspace namespace (likely fleet-default) and a stable label to target (e.g. management.cattle.io/cluster-name=c-nnzn9) 90 ``` 91 92 **Acceptance:** `GET /v1/fleet.cattle.io.gitrepos` returns 200 (CRD present), and the lab cluster is listed under a Fleet workspace with a targetable label. 93 94 --- 95 96 ## Phase 1 — Make lab-fleet deployable (owner: agent-executable; per-component) 97 98 Add a `fleet.yaml` to each numbered dir. Two shapes: 99 100 **Helm-backed component** (`01-networking`, `02-longhorn`, `03-gpu` nvdp, `04-ollama`, `05-open-webui`, `06-milvus`): 101 ```yaml 102 # 04-ollama/fleet.yaml 103 defaultNamespace: ai 104 helm: 105 releaseName: ollama 106 repo: https://otwld.github.io/ollama-helm/ 107 chart: ollama 108 version: 1.60.0 # pin to the running chart (from helm/releases.json) 109 valuesFiles: 110 - values.yaml 111 takeOwnership: true # adopt the hand-installed release (see risk note) 112 ``` 113 114 **Raw-manifest component** (`03-gpu` runtimeclass, `07-comfyui`, `09-mcp`, `10-hermes`…`13-resilio`, netpol): 115 ```yaml 116 # 07-comfyui/fleet.yaml 117 defaultNamespace: ai 118 # no helm block → Fleet applies the YAML in this path as-is 119 ``` 120 121 Notes: 122 - Secrets stay out of git (already `secretKeyRef`/`.example`); create them out-of-band before the bundle lands, or Fleet will report the workload as not-ready. 123 - Pin every chart `version` to what's live (`lab-capture/.../helm/releases.json`) so Fleet adopts rather than upgrades. 124 - `00-host` and `14-cluster-mgmt` are documentation, not deployables — exclude via the GitRepo `paths` (below), don't add `fleet.yaml`. 125 126 **Acceptance (per dir):** `fleet.yaml` present; `kubeconform`/`helm template` dry-run clean. watch for pod churn | 172 | 4 | `01-networking`, `03-gpu` | helm adopt | med–high | MetalLB/Traefik + GPU; network blips possible | 173 | 5 | `02-longhorn` | helm adopt | **high** | storage — adopt last, or leave monitor-only | 174 175 At each step: add the path, let Fleet render, confirm the BundleDeployment goes Ready, spot-check the workload, then proceed. Roll back by removing the path (Fleet reverts its bundle) or deleting the GitRepo. 176 177 --- 178 179 ## Phase 4 — Verify (either credential works) 180 181 ```bash 182 # controller view 183 curl -sk https://rancher.ash4d.com/v1/fleet.cattle.io.gitrepos/fleet-default/lab-fleet \ 184 -H "Authorization: Bearer $RTOKEN" | jq '.status.summary' 185 # downstream view (using the k3s kubeconfig) 186 kubectl --context lab -n cattle-fleet-system get bundledeployments -A 187 ``` 188 Confirm: bundles Ready, no unexpected restarts on adopted releases, GPU/storage healthy. 189 190 --- 191 192 ## Risks & mitigations 193 194 - **Helm adoption is the real hazard.** Fleet taking ownership of a hand-installed release can trigger a redeploy if the rendered manifest differs from what's live. *Mitigation:* pin chart versions to the running ones, diff `helm get manifest` vs `helm template` before enabling a helm path, and adopt one release at a time. Longhorn last. 195 - **Fleet feature currently looks off** (`fleet=false` hint). *Mitigation:* Phase 0 verifies and enables on the controller. 196 - **Submodule trap.** A GitRepo on `ash4d.com/lab` clones an empty gitlink. *Mitigation:* target `lab-fleet` directly (decision #1). 197 - **Missing secrets = stuck bundles.** Raw manifests reference `secretKeyRef`. *Mitigation:* create the referenced secrets out-of-band before adding those paths. 198 - **Sandbox can't touch the controller.** All controller-side steps are Rick-run (or agent-run) with a Rancher token; I can drive downstream verification with the k3s kubeconfig. 199 200 ## What I can do next (with a Rancher API token) 201 202 Given a Rancher token for `.148`, I can run Phase 0 discovery, author all the `fleet.yaml` files (Phase 1) as a ticket batch on the lab-fleet board, and generate the exact GitRepo payload — leaving only the approve-and-widen calls to you. Without the token, Phases 0/2 are yours; Phase 1 I can do now against the repo. 203 204 ## Portfolio payoff 205 206 Completing this converts the lab from "config-as-code, published" to "**GitOps-reconciled from git by Fleet on a live multi-workload cluster, with a staged Helm-adoption playbook**" — a materially stronger line, and a natural companion writeup to the NemoClaw build (which watches this same Fleet delivery pipeline). 207

This is the still-open half of the scope doc's A/B question and the stronger portfolio line ("and Fleet deploys from this repo"). --- ## Discovered state (live, 2026-07-06) Pulled read-only from the downstream API (`192.168.7.149:6443`, `system:admin` cert) and the cluster-agent env. | Fact | Value | Source | |---|---|---| | Rancher server | `v2.14.2` at `https://rancher.ash4d.com` (**192.168.7.148** — separate host) | cattle-cluster-agent env `CATTLE_SERVER` / `CATTLE_SERVER_VERSION` | | ash4d lab node | `sdf1`, k3s `v1.35.5+k3s1`, single node, `node-role…control-plane=true` | `/version`, `/api/v1/nodes` | | Cluster role | **Downstream** of Rancher (cluster id `c-nnzn9`), cattle-cluster-agent + fleet-agent connected | helm release `fleet-agent-c-nnzn9`, cluster-agent env | | fleet-agent | `rancher/fleet-agent:v0.15.2`, Deployment **Ready 1/1** | `cattle-fleet-system` deploys | | **Active GitOps here** | **None** — no `fleet.cattle.io` CRDs on the downstream, no `cluster-fleet-*` namespaces, no BundleDeployments | `/apis/fleet.cattle.io/v1alpha1` → 404; CRD + namespace lists | | Feature hint | cluster-agent env shows `CATTLE_FEATURES=…fleet=false…` | cattle-cluster-agent env | **Read:** the cluster is registered to Rancher and the fleet-agent is healthy, but Continuous Delivery has never been wired — no repo deploys here. GitRepo/Bundle objects live on the **controller** (the Rancher local cluster, `.148`), which is why none appear on the downstream. > ⚠ **Assumption flagged:** `fleet=false` in the *agent* env is a strong hint that Continuous Delivery is disabled globally, but the authoritative flag lives on the controller (`.148`), which I can't reach without a Rancher token. Phase 0 verifies it before anything else. --- ## Topology ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph mgmt["Rancher server — 192.168.7.148 (rancher.ash4d.com)"] RANCHER["Rancher v2.14.2
local cluster"] FC["Fleet controller
+ gitjob"] GR["GitRepo CR
(fleet-default)"] RANCHER --- FC --- GR end subgraph down["ash4d lab — 192.168.7.149 (sdf1, downstream c-nnzn9)"] AGENT["fleet-agent v0.15.2
(Ready, idle today)"] BD["BundleDeployments
(none yet)"] WL["cluster workloads
ai / longhorn / etc."] AGENT --> BD --> WL end GH[("GitHub
darthzen/lab-fleet")] GR -- "clones + renders" --> GH FC == "pushes Bundles over the
registered agent tunnel" ==> AGENT ``` --- ## Key design decisions (made, with rationale) 1. **Point the GitRepo at `lab-fleet` directly — not `ash4d.com/lab`.** `ash4d.com` mounts `lab-fleet` as a git *submodule*; Fleet's git clone does not initialise submodules reliably, so a GitRepo on `ash4d.com/lab` would clone an empty gitlink. Targeting `lab-fleet` directly sidesteps this. `ash4d.com` stays the documentation showcase; `lab-fleet` is the deploy source. (This resolves the scope doc's A/B toward "split, deploy from lab-fleet.") 2. **Public repo → no clone secret.** `lab-fleet` is public, so the GitRepo needs no SSH/HTTP credential. One less secret on the controller. 3. **Stage by risk; adopt hand-installed Helm releases last.** Everything in `lab-fleet` was hand-installed. Fleet taking over a live Helm release is the only genuinely risky step (ownership handoff, possible pod churn). Raw-manifest components go first; storage/GPU/critical infra goes last or stays monitor-only. 4. **`lab-fleet` needs `fleet.yaml` files to be deployable.** Today it holds helm *values* and raw manifests, not Fleet bundles. Each component dir needs a `fleet.yaml` (helm chart+version+valuesFiles, or a raw-manifest bundle). Give it a minute to reconcile. 3. **Confirm the cluster's Fleet identity + labels** (for targeting): ```bash curl -sk https://rancher.ash4d.com/v1/fleet.cattle.io.clusters \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $RTOKEN" | jq '.data[]|{ns:.metadata.namespace,name:.metadata.name,labels:.metadata.labels,cluster:.status.agentDeployedComponents}' # note the workspace namespace (likely fleet-default) and a stable label to target (e.g. management.cattle.io/cluster-name=c-nnzn9) ``` **Acceptance:** `GET /v1/fleet.cattle.io.gitrepos` returns 200 (CRD present), and the lab cluster is listed under a Fleet workspace with a targetable label. --- ## Phase 1 — Make lab-fleet deployable (owner: agent-executable; per-component) Add a `fleet.yaml` to each numbered dir. # Fleet bundle — MetalLB (Helm). See fleet-wiring-plan Phase 1.
# Adopts the hand-installed release; pinned to the running chart so Fleet does
# not upgrade on adoption. IPAddressPool/L2Advertisement are a separate bundle
# under pools/ (they need the MetalLB CRDs to exist first).
# traefik-values.yaml is NOT bundled — Traefik is k3s-bundled and managed via

defaultNamespace: metallb-system
helm:
  releaseName: metallb
  repo: https://metallb.github.io/metallb
  chart: metallb
  version: "0.16.1"
  valuesFiles:
    - metallb-values.yaml
  takeOwnership: true

---
name: cowork-handoff-to-claude-code
description: Division of labor — Cowork writes the handoff/plan, Claude Code does the in-repo writes and commits
metadata:
  type: feedback
---

For lab repos, a Claude Cowork session (sandboxed, in Dropbox) should produce a
**handoff/plan document only** — it must NOT write changes into a working copy.
The actual file writes and git commits are done by Claude Code running in the
real repo, so git metadata (authorship, rename history, clean staged diffs) is
preserved.

**Why:** Cowork wrote fleet.yaml files into a throwaway `ash4d-lab-draft/` tree
instead of handing off; Rick reverted them and had it write the plan
(`fleet-wiring-plan.md`) so the writes/commits could go through the proper path
here.

**How to apply:** When told to "read the handoff and execute," treat the doc as
the spec, ground every value against the *current* real repo (not the month-old
draft — chart versions drift), do the writes with `git mv`/Write in-repo, and

This cluster runs as a Rancher downstream: `cattle-cluster-agent` (Rancher
v2.14.2) connects out to the Rancher server, which installed and manages
`rancher-webhook`, `system-upgrade-controller`, Fleet (`fleet-agent` v0.15.2),
and the `rancher-monitoring` stack (kube-prometheus 109.0.2 + Grafana
dashboards + DCGM GPU metrics). These components are lifecycle-managed by

# Fleet Wiring — Handoff / Resume Notes

Turning `lab-fleet` from published reference config into **GitOps that Rancher
Fleet deploys** to this cluster. Full plan (private): Cowork workspace
`_portfolio/ash4d-lab/fleet-wiring-plan.md`. This file is the short resume note
for picking the work back up.

## Topology

- **Rancher controller** — `v2.14.2` at `https://rancher.ash4d.com`
  (**192.168.7.148**, separate host). GitRepo/Bundle objects live here.
- **This cluster** — `sdf1`, downstream `c-nnzn9` at **192.168.7.149**.
  `fleet-agent v0.15.2` is Ready but idle — no GitRepo targets it yet.
- Deploy source is **`lab-fleet` directly**, not `ash4d.com/lab` (Fleet doesn't
  init git submodules, so a GitRepo on `ash4d.com` would clone an empty gitlink).
  Public repo → no clone secret needed.

## ✅ Phase 1 — DONE (committed to `main`, not yet pushed)

Every deployable numbered dir has a `fleet.yaml`:

- **Helm bundles** (`takeOwnership: true`, versions pinned to the running
  releases so Fleet adopts instead of upgrading):
  metallb `0.16.1`, longhorn `1.12.0`, nvdp `0.19.2`, dcgm-exporter `4.8.2`,
  ollama `1.67.0` (confirmed live), open-webui `14.8.0`, milvus `5.0.22`.
- **Raw-manifest bundles:** comfyui, indexer, mcp, hermes, emby, node-red,
  resilio, plus metallb pools, the nvidia RuntimeClass, and attu.
- Where a dir had a chart **and** raw manifests, the raw manifests were moved
  into a sibling subdir so each chart owns its own bundle path (Fleet = one
  chart per path): `01-networking/pools/`, `03-gpu/{dcgm-exporter,runtimeclass}/`,
  `06-milvus/attu/`.
- `09-mcp/github-mcp-token.secret.example.yaml` → `.yaml.txt` so Fleet never
  applies the placeholder Secret.
- `00-host` and `14-cluster-mgmt` stay docs-only (no `fleet.yaml`). Traefik is
  k3s-bundled and not Fleet-managed (`traefik-values.yaml` is reference only).

**Not yet pushed** — push `lab-fleet` `main` to the remote before Fleet can
clone it.

## ⏭ Phase 0 — Enable CD on the controller (needs Rancher API token for .148)

1. Mint a Rancher API token (UI: avatar → Account & API Keys → Create API Key,
   no scope), export as `RTOKEN`.
2. Check the `fleet` feature (agent env hinted `fleet=false`); enable if off:
   `PUT https://rancher.ash4d.com/v3/features/fleet -d '{"value":true}'`.
3. Confirm the cluster's Fleet workspace (likely `fleet-default`) and a stable
   target label (e.g. `management.cattle.io/cluster-name: c-nnzn9`).
- **Acceptance:** `GET /v1/fleet.cattle.io.gitrepos` returns 200 and the cluster
  is listed under a Fleet workspace with a targetable label.

## ⏭ Phase 2/3 — GitRepo + staged rollout (needs token; approve each widen)

Create a GitRepo in the Fleet workspace pointing at
`https://github.com/darthzen/lab-fleet`, branch `main`. **Start narrow** with a
single low-risk `paths:` entry, then widen one component at a time, riskiest
last:

| Order | Component(s) | Risk | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `12-node-red`, `13-resilio`, `11-emby` | low | app layer, easy rollback |
| 2 | `07-comfyui`, `09-mcp`, `10-hermes` | low–med | Secrets must pre-exist |
| 3 | `05-open-webui`, `06-milvus`, `04-ollama` | med | helm adopt; watch pod churn |
| 4 | `01-networking`, `03-gpu` | med–high | MetalLB/Traefik + GPU; net blips |
| 5 | `02-longhorn` | **high** | storage — adopt last, or monitor-only |

At each step: add the path, let Fleet render, confirm the BundleDeployment goes
Ready, spot-check the workload, proceed. Roll back by removing the path.

## Watch-outs

- **Helm adoption is the real hazard.** `helm get manifest` vs `helm template`
  should match before enabling a helm path; adopt one release at a time.
- **Missing Secrets = stuck bundles.** Raw manifests use `secretKeyRef`
  (github-mcp token, open-webui `WEBUI_SECRET_KEY`, hermes Slack/API). Create
  them out-of-band before adding those paths.
- Verify: controller `…/gitrepos/fleet-default/lab-fleet | jq .status.summary`;
  downstream `kubectl -n cattle-fleet-system get bundledeployments -A`.

# lab-fleet — Home Lab Cluster Configuration as Code The complete, install-ordered configuration of a single-node k3s cluster running a private AI platform: local LLM inference on a Tesla V100, RAG over Milvus, agentic MCP tooling, GPU image generation, and the home services that share the node. Every file was curated from **live cluster state** — helm values pulled from running releases, manifests reconstructed from applied objects, and application sources recovered from container image layers after their Dockerfiles were lost. This repo is mounted as the [`lab/` submodule of ash4d.com](https://github.com/darthzen/ash4d.com), which carries the full architecture documentation. This repo carries the configs. ## Installation Order ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph host["00 — Host (openSUSE Leap 16.0)"] DRIVER[NVIDIA G06 driver + CDI] K3S[k3s v1.35] end subgraph platform["01-03 — Platform"] METALLB[01 MetalLB
